reasoning: Confirmed real. get_customizer_data() at line 89-111 of customizer.php calls Utils\get_google_api_key() which returns get_option('tt-font-google-api-key', '') — a raw unescaped DB value. This value is passed directly into wp_localize_script() twice: (1) under customize_controls_enqueue_scripts hook as the egfCustomize JS global (controls panel), and (2) under customize_preview_init hook as the egfCustomizePreview JS global (preview iframe). Both embed the raw API key in inline JavaScript that is visible in page source and the JS console. No capability check exists within the plugin's callback functions; the plugin relies solely on WordPress core's built-in Customizer gate (the 'customize' capability, which maps to 'edit_theme_options' and is admin-only by default, but can be granted to editors by themes/plugins). Severity is correctly low: the 'customize' capability limits exposure to privileged users, Google Fonts API keys are low-value credentials (quota-abuse risk only, not secret/auth credentials), and some client-side exposure may be partially by design since the browser JS needs the key to call the Google Fonts API. No sanitizer or redaction guard was missed.
